title: RA.1034 RamonPang

translation.content: Ramon Tambucon, AKA RamonPang, takes his work seriously. The LA-based DJ and producer has become Gen Z's de facto electronic music historian, with a TikTok channel that's a treasure trove for stories from the past, present and future. He's just as happy discussing Mark Fisher as Skrillex—we dare you to find someone who can make a discussion of Autechre's gear more exciting. Today, Pang's posts clock up thousands of views. He's even landed on Forbes, explaining the connection between IDM and trap. But Pang's relationship with music goes beyond content. His creative platform, The Tabula Rasa Record Company, is a hub for editorial, releases and live showcases. Run alongside Jozef White, it's been instrumental in documenting burgeoning scenes like UK garage in California. Pang also makes music, and while he may be serious about the craft, with a fine-tuned era for flair, his tunes are also very fun. His approach in the booth and the studio borders on the ecstatic. When his own "Forest Volt" drops early in his RA Mix, the track's cartoonish melody sounds like it's about to wiggle its way out of the stereo to paint you in neon. Pang keeps the rest of RA.1034 just as bright and buoyant, tracing quick lines through IDM, electro, acid and bassy music—from internet-native newcomers like Kooxla to ’90s Belgian maestro Gerome Sportelli and, of course, Burial. At points, Pang's mixing seems to defy physics: bird chirps, flutes, cascading chords and harps flare like cosmic fireworks over heavy low-end and lightning-fast breaks, as if the space-time continuum is being rearranged in real time. – Henry Ivry What have you been up to recently? I'm currently back home in Los Angeles after doing a short Japan tour with my good friend Ninajirachi a couple weeks ago. Just being around her and her team, seeing all the passion and energy in another country (shoutout to Heaven's Gate Crew and Tabula Rasa Records) was really inspiring. I was also virtually attending the School of Song lectures hosted by Oneohtrix Point Never while in Japan, and hearing him talk about musical identity and artist psychobiography has had my brain really swirling. After all the short-form reels and videos about IDM, Aphex Twin and niche electronic music started getting more attention in the last year, I've been thinking about how I've earned a lot of people's trust. It's funny because I learned all these fun tidbits about music because it informs my DJing and my music production. When I know the history of IDM or Filipinos' contribution to electronic music, I feel like I'm participating in an ongoing canon by making music that feels right to me. Therefore, if I'm going to DJ and make music from now on, it needs to be with the same level of intention as these artists I'm covering and discussing. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This was recorded at home in Los Angeles. It was mixed on three CDJs with a DJM A9 using 44.1 kHz AIF files. The goal of my RA Mix was seeing how far I can push the groove in the mix while still playing as much progressive electronic music as I can. It's really difficult music to mix (especially the Gescom, James Ferraro and Si Begg tracks, for example), and you can definitely hear me sweating and struggling to beatmatch at points, but I kept it in because it sounds very live. For genres, it's mainly IDM, electro, acid—records from my friends at Tabula Rasa—but the sense of melody or 'song' keeps everything tied together thematically. I also love organic instruments like marimbas, harps and flutes and I try to include those as much as possible in mixes. It's very 'me.' What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm really looking forward to playing at Parameter 2026 in San Francisco in May. I'm playing on the same day as Dopplereffekt, Andy Stott, Polygonia, Cousin and D.Dan. A dream come true. I also have plans to do more solo, curated shows in Los Angeles and other cities this year. Stay tuned for that. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The Philippines has a lot of eyes on it right now geographically, but we cannot continue being a weapons factory for the US while journalists are being shot in the streets. Authoritarianism and dictatorship still plagues my family in the Philippines and the US continues to back it. The Philippine Human Rights Act (PHRA) is a call to suspend US security assistance to the Philippines until human rights violations by Philippine security forces cease. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I'll pick two in Los Angeles, since they've affected me in similar ways. First: Scenario at The Love Song Bar. It's a free party (used to be weekly, now monthly) run by Daddy Kev and Mapamota. I only got to attend one Low End Theory party before it closed, and even the psychedelic, genre-mixing, head-nodding remnants of that scene leaking into this successor party have been incredibly inspiring. I enjoy how open people are there. People are there to dance, club and have a good time, but it’s not an out-of-place moment when Daddy Kev drops Squarepusher's "Come On My Selector" before DJ Lag comes on, for example. A level of electronic music with more progression and weird ideas is just understood there, and knowing that exists lets me be more wild with my ideas. Second: Brownies & Lemonade (B&L). While I was in college, I had the fire of festival EDM trap music inside of me, and I was also being opened up to the wilder sounds of SOPHIE, X&G, Sinjin Hawke, at the time through Soundcloud. B&L was the only party around that fit that niche. There's a level of screaming euphoria you get at EDM shows with loud systems that you don't get anywhere else. I don't think I'd have my appreciation for both sides of the dance music spectrum without all their events. I'm still an EDM trap oldhead at heart, and you can hear it in the fills and flourishes of my music and the way I DJ.
translation.blurb: A two-hour sprint through maximalist IDM and acid from the Filipino DJ, producer and lore magnet.
